title: ExPUNations: Augmenting Puns with Keywords and Explanations
research_questions: How can an existing pun dataset be augmented with fine-grained annotations of understandability, offensiveness, joke intent, funniness, natural-language explanations, and joke keywords?
Can natural-language explanations be generated for puns, and can explanations improve models' ability to classify whether a text is a joke?
Can human-annotated pun keywords improve keyword-conditioned pun generation compared with automatically extracted keywords and an AmbiPun baseline?
How well do current pretrained NLP models perform on pun explanation and keyword-conditioned pun generation?

datasets_used.name: SemEval-2017 Task 7 puns
datasets_used.size: 834 heterographic puns, 1,074 homographic puns and 91 non-puns

datasets_used.name: BookCorpus
datasets_used.size: 200 sentences that contain the pun word for a given pun word

methods_proposed.name: ExPUN annotation schema
methods_proposed.summary: The paper augments SemEval 2017 Task 7 samples with six annotation fields: understandability, offensiveness, joke intent, 1-5 funniness, natural-language explanations, and joke keywords.

methods_proposed.name: Keyword aggregation algorithm
methods_proposed.summary: The algorithm merges five annotators' keyword lists by selecting high-reliability anchor annotators, fuzzy-matching keyword phrases with a threshold of 60, and choosing concise consensus keywords.

methods_proposed.name: Pun explanation for classification
methods_proposed.summary: The paper formulates pun explanation as generating a natural-language explanation from a pun sentence, then evaluates explanation utility through pun classification using simulatability: acc(IE → O) − acc(I → O).

methods_proposed.name: Keyword-conditioned pun generation
methods_proposed.summary: The paper defines a generation task in which models receive human-annotated keywords, a pun word, an alternate pun word, and optionally pun-word senses, then generate a novel pun incorporating them.

methods_proposed.name: T5PT+FT
methods_proposed.summary: The generation model first pretrains T5-base on BookCorpus sentences paired with RAKE-extracted keywords and then finetunes on ExPUN keyword-to-pun examples.

humor_theories.detail: The related work section says human-extracted keywords usually reflect the ambiguity and distinctiveness principles discussed in Kao et al. (2016), motivating keyword-conditioned pun generation; the principles are not formalized as the annotation scheme.

evaluation_methods.detail: A professional team of 10 dedicated full-time annotators provided 5 annotations per sample, after a kick-off meeting, 3 pilot rounds, and 7 rounds of annotation with manual quality checks.
Dataset agreement was measured with averaged Cohen's kappa and Spearman's rho for numeric fields, and pairwise BLEU-4 and METEOR for explanation and keyword text fields.
Pun explanation quality was evaluated by simulatability, measuring joke-classification accuracy improvement when explanations are added to the input.
Keyword-conditioned pun generation was evaluated with word incorporation rate for pun words and keywords.

Amazon Mechanical Turk workers labeled whether generated puns were successful; workers had to pass a qualifier by correctly labeling >= 80% of 20 manually annotated samples, each item had 3 annotators, and Fleiss' kappa was κ = 0.49.

headline_results.finding: The ExPUN dataset contains 1,999 samples, with majority-vote labels identifying 1,795 understandable samples, 65 offensive/inappropriate samples, and 1,449 jokes, and the average funniness score is 1.68.
ExPUN includes 6,650 natural-language explanations, averaging 3.33 explanations per sample, 31.67 tokens per explanation, and 2.01 sentences per explanation.
Keyword annotations showed much higher lexical agreement than explanations.
For numeric annotation fields, the highest reported Cohen's kappa was for whether the text is a joke.
With ExPUN keywords, T5PT+FT achieved the highest human-evaluated pun generation success rate.
With ExPUN keywords, AmbiPun achieved the highest word incorporation rates but a lower human success rate than T5PT+FT.
headline_results.metric: Word Incorp. % p_w / K / both; Success Rate %

key_findings: ExPUN adds fine-grained humor annotations to SemEval 2017 Task 7 puns, including natural-language explanations and sparse joke keywords.
Annotators showed higher agreement on keyword spans than on free-form explanations, while joke intent had the strongest numeric-field kappa at 0.58.
Gold human-written explanations are useful for pun classification, and the paper reports that adding randomly sampled negative explanations further improves classification accuracy.
T5-generated explanations often identify the pun word but usually fail to explain the alternate word sense or phonological similarity needed for the wordplay.
Human-annotated ExPUN keywords led to more successful generated puns than RAKE-extracted keywords: T5PT+FT reached 77.0 success with ExPUN keywords versus 54.0 with RAKE keywords.
AmbiPun incorporated input words best under ExPUN keywords, but this did not translate into the highest human-rated pun success.
limitations: The work focuses on puns, a single and very specific form of humorous language.
Humor is highly subjective, and perceptions of humor can differ greatly depending on annotators' backgrounds and experiences.
Generated explanations did not improve classification accuracy at test time, and qualitative analysis found that T5 often failed to explain alternate senses or phonological similarity.
The pun generation model can fail to incorporate the alternate word sense meaningfully and can copy input keywords into incoherent sentences.

Because the generation systems use pretrained language models, they are susceptible to generating biased or sensitive content, and the paper does not explicitly address those concerns within the framework.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper states that no user data from commercial voice assistant systems is used; the source text comes from SemEval 2017 Task 7. Annotators were full-time employees with benefits, personal worker information was discarded, and pay per task was above the annotator's local minimum wage, approximately $15 USD / Hour. The authors acknowledge ACL Code of Ethics compliance and note that pretrained generation models may produce biased or sensitive content, which they do not explicitly address to date.

human_samples.population: professional team of dedicated full-time annotators within the coauthors’ organization; not coauthors; full-time employees with benefits
human_samples.recruitment: hired by the coauthors’ organization for data annotation; received a kick-off meeting, 3 pilot calibration rounds, and then worked through 7 annotation rounds with quality feedback and redoing batches as necessary
human_samples.observations: 5 annotations per sample for 1,999 SemEval-derived text samples; annotation fields covered understandability, offensiveness/inappropriateness, joke intent, 1–5 funniness, natural-language explanations, and joke keywords; dataset includes 6,650 explanations
human_samples.detail: Each minibatch was annotated by consistent subteams of 5 annotators. Annotators could skip downstream fields if they did not understand the text or did not judge it to be a joke. Personal worker information was discarded; pay per task was above local minimum wage, approximately $15/hour.

human_samples.recruitment: Turkers had to pass a qualifier by correctly labeling >= 80% of 20 manually annotated samples
human_samples.observations: 3 annotators per generated pun; workers labeled whether each generated pun was successful; Fleiss’ kappa reported as κ = 0.49
human_samples.detail: Success was defined as whether the text supports both senses of the pun word. The paper does not report the number of unique Turkers, their demographics, geography, or whether they were blinded to model/condition.

human_samples.observations: 20 samples were manually annotated for the AMT qualifier; completed professional-annotator batches were manually examined for quality and sometimes redone
human_samples.detail: The paper says the authors/manual reviewers provided quality feedback and manually annotated qualifier examples, but does not identify the individuals or count them.

judges.name: professional full-time annotators
judges.detail: Provided the ExPUN annotations. AF1–AF3 dataset labels were summarized by majority vote; AF4 used nonblank funniness scores; AF5 and AF6 supplied explanations and keyword phrases.

judges.name: paper team / manual reviewers
judges.detail: Manually inspected completed annotation batches, gave feedback on quality issues, had batches redone as necessary, and manually annotated the 20-sample AMT qualifier set.

judges.name: Amazon Mechanical Turk workers
judges.detail: Primary human judge for generated-pun success rates in the keyword-conditioned pun generation evaluation; 3 annotators per generated pun.

judges.name: classification accuracy / simulatability score
judges.detail: Pun explanation utility was evaluated through acc(IE → O) − acc(I → O), using trained classifiers and reporting three-seed average accuracy curves.

judges.name: word incorporation rate
judges.detail: Automatic metric for keyword-conditioned pun generation, measuring incorporation of pun words, keywords, and both.

judges.name: Cohen’s kappa, Spearman’s rho, BLEU-4, METEOR, Fleiss’ kappa
judges.detail: Used to quantify annotation agreement for ExPUN fields and AMT human-evaluation agreement.

judges.name: keyword aggregation algorithm with reliability score and fuzzy matching
judges.detail: Aggregated five annotators’ keyword lists into a single keyword set per sample using an anchor annotator selected by reliability score and fuzzy matching with threshold 60.

generation_selection.detail: Appendix E states that T5 inference used beam search with beam size 2 and stopped at EOS or maximum target length; one generated explanation was then used for the downstream explanation-augmented classifier.

generation_selection.scope: T5FT and T5PT+FT keyword-conditioned pun generation
generation_selection.detail: The T5-based pun generators used beam search with beam size 2 at inference; the paper does not report temperature, top-p, or any human/manual post-selection.
The paper reports the AmbiPun prompt format and that no further finetuning was used, but does not report decoding parameters, number of candidates, or any selection procedure.

model_provenance.name: BERT-base
model_provenance.detail: Finetuned via HuggingFace for pun classification with and without explanations. No exact checkpoint revision, software version, training date, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: RoBERTa-base
model_provenance.detail: Finetuned via HuggingFace for pun classification with and without explanations. No exact checkpoint revision, software version, training date, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: DeBERTa-base
model_provenance.detail: Finetuned via HuggingFace for pun classification with and without explanations. No exact checkpoint revision, software version, training date, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: T5-base
model_provenance.detail: Used through SimpleT5 for explanation generation and for T5FT/T5PT+FT keyword-conditioned pun generation. No exact checkpoint revision, SimpleT5 version, training date, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: ELV / ELV-sa
model_provenance.detail: The authors used released ELV code and inherited most default hyperparameters for ELV-sa, changing training batch size per GPU to 4. Exact code commit/checkpoint and inference date are not reported.

model_provenance.name: AmbiPun
model_provenance.detail: Used as the state-of-the-art homographic pun generation baseline with no further finetuning. The paper gives the prompt format but not the checkpoint, code version, decoding setup, or inference date.

model_provenance.name: roberta-large-mnli
model_provenance.detail: Mentioned in Appendix D implementation details with reduced training epochs and batch size; the paper does not clearly tie this model to main reported results.

model_provenance.name: bart-large-mnli
model_provenance.detail: Mentioned in Appendix D implementation details with reduced training epochs and batch size; the paper does not clearly tie this model to main reported results.

inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Hyperparameter search tried learning rates {1e-4, 3e-4, 1e-5, 3e-5} × training epochs {3, 10, 20}. Final hyperparameters for bert-base, roberta-base, and deberta-base: learning rate 1e-5, 20 epochs, batch size 32. Best dev-accuracy checkpoint chosen for inference; results reported as three-seed averages.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: HuggingFace used; classifier hardware and cost not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: Pun explanation/classification split was 1,699 train / 100 dev / 200 test, with dev and test balanced between jokes and non-jokes.

inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Training epochs reduced to 3 and training batch size to 8.
inference_budgets.detail: The appendix reports these settings but does not clearly describe the models’ role in the main experiments or provide results.

inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Used released ELV code with most default ELV-sa hyperparameters; training batch size per GPU changed to 4.
inference_budgets.detail: No token limits, number of inference calls, GPU count, or cost are reported.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: one generated explanation used per item; number of model calls not explicitly reported
inference_budgets.token_budget: maximum source length 512; maximum target length 256
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: T5-base from SimpleT5; AdamW learning rate 0.0001; batch size 8; 30 finetuning epochs; beam search decoding with beam size 2; decoding stopped at EOS or maximum target length.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Trained on a Tesla V100 for finetuning; cost not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: For samples with no annotated explanation, the pun sentence itself was used as the output explanation during data preparation.

inference_budgets.scope: T5FT and T5PT+FT keyword-conditioned pun generation
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: one generated pun used per test input; number of model calls not explicitly reported
inference_budgets.token_budget: maximum source length 512; maximum target length 256
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: T5-base from SimpleT5; AdamW learning rate 0.0001; batch size 8; 30 finetuning epochs; beam search decoding with beam size 2. T5PT+FT additionally pretrained for 3 epochs on BookCorpus-derived non-pun examples before ExPUN finetuning.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Finetuning used a Tesla V100; cost not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: Generation data was limited to 1,482 samples with ExPUN keywords and SemEval pun-word sense annotations; 100 were reserved as test data and the rest used for training. For BookCorpus pretraining, the authors extracted 200 sentences containing the pun word for a given pun word and used RAKE keywords.
The paper reports no finetuning and the prompt format “generate sentence: K, p_w, a_w,” but not decoding settings, candidate count, hardware, or cost.

inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: RAKE was run on pun text for automatically extracted keyword training/evaluation conditions and on BookCorpus sentences for T5PT+FT pretraining examples.
inference_budgets.detail: No RAKE implementation version, parameter settings, runtime, or cost are reported.

human_baselines.scope: Pun explanation utility for pun classification
human_baselines.description: Gold human-written ExPUN explanations were used as an upper-bound explanation condition at training and testing time, and compared against no-explanation and T5-generated-explanation conditions in the same classifier-based simulatability framework.
human_baselines.detail: This is a human-explanation baseline/upper bound, not a human pun-classification performance baseline. The paper selected the longest human-written explanation per sample and substituted the pun text when no explanation existed.

human_baselines.description: Human majority vote supplied labels, but the paper does not report human classifier accuracy on the same train/dev/test task.
human_baselines.detail: Annotator agreement is reported, but not a directly matched human-vs-model classification baseline.

human_baselines.description: The paper does not evaluate human-written puns generated from the same keyword, pun-word, and alternate-word inputs under the same AMT success protocol.
human_baselines.detail: Original SemEval puns are source/reference material and training targets, but not a directly evaluated human generation baseline.

mitigations: ExPUN annotations were newly collected for this paper rather than copied from the original SemEval task.
The authors used train/dev/test splits for pun explanation/classification and held out 100 test examples for keyword-conditioned pun generation.
Models were finetuned on designated training splits rather than intentionally trained on the held-out test split.
contamination_risk.basis: The source texts were sampled from the public SemEval 2017 Task 7 dataset, while the evaluated pretrained models/checkpoints such as BERT, RoBERTa, T5, DeBERTa, and AmbiPun postdate or could postdate the public release of those puns. The paper does not discuss contamination or run a decontamination test. The newly collected ExPUN annotations reduce but do not eliminate risk, because the underlying pun texts were public legacy benchmark material.

llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: Generated-pun success was judged by AMT workers; dataset labels and explanations/keywords came from professional annotators. No LLM evaluator is reported.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: BERT/RoBERTa/DeBERTa/T5/ELV/AmbiPun are systems under test or trained task models, not LLM judges. Automatic metrics include classification accuracy, simulatability, word incorporation, and agreement scores, but no LLM-as-judge.

reporting_gaps: No demographic, native-language, or geographic information is reported for the 10 professional annotators or for AMT workers.
The number of unique AMT workers is not reported; only 3 annotators per generated pun is specified.
The AMT evaluation does not report whether workers were blinded to model identity/condition, how items were randomized, or exactly how the 3 labels per item were aggregated into success rates.
The paper does not explicitly state the total number of generated items or total AMT judgments per model/condition, although the generation test set size is given as 100.
Exact model checkpoint revisions, code commits, library versions, training dates, and inference dates are not reported for HuggingFace, SimpleT5, ELV, or AmbiPun models.
AmbiPun decoding parameters, number of generated candidates, token limits, hardware, and inference cost are not reported.
T5 decoding reports beam size and length limits, but not seed values, length penalties, temperature/top-p settings, or whether any postprocessing/filtering was applied.
Classifier inference token limits, hardware, GPU count, and cost are not reported; T5 hardware is only reported as a Tesla V100 for finetuning.
The paper reports three-seed averages for classification figures but does not provide the seed values.
The paper does not discuss benchmark contamination or decontamination despite using public SemEval 2017 source texts with later pretrained models.
The roles of roberta-large-mnli and bart-large-mnli mentioned in Appendix D are not clearly connected to the main reported experimental results.
